era_context: The Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin is in the midst of its territorial expansion and consolidation. In Egypt, Pepi II's reign continues during the late Old Kingdom, characterized by increasing provincial autonomy. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature phase. In China, the Xia Dynasty (if historical) would be in its early phase, though this remains archaeologically unconfirmed.

events.title: Naram-Sin's military campaigns in Anatolia
events.description: Naram-Sin campaigns in Anatolia, particularly against the Lullubi and other highland peoples. These campaigns are documented in inscriptions and represent an attempt to extend Akkadian control beyond Mesopotamia proper. The campaigns are militarily successful but logistically challenging and contribute to the strain on Akkadian resources. The Victory Stele of Naram-Sin, though not dated to a specific year, commemorates such campaigns.

sources.name: Naram-Sin's inscriptions and the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's Anatolian campaigns are well-attested in inscriptions. The specific year -2332 is assigned as a representative year within his reign; individual campaigns are not dated to specific years.

events.title: Pepi II's reign: Continued administrative decentralization
events.description: Pepi II's reign continues to be marked by the rise of provincial governors and the weakening of central authority. The pharaoh's power is increasingly challenged by nomarchs who control local resources and military forces. This period sees the construction of elaborate private tombs and the decline in royal building projects, reflecting the shift in power from the center to the provinces.
key_figures: Pepi II (Pharaoh of Egypt, Dynasty VI)
events.certainty_note: The general trend of administrative decentralization is well-documented. Specific events of -2332 are not individually recorded.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: Urban centers flourish
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization's major urban centers (Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, and others) are at their peak of development. These cities feature sophisticated urban planning, including grid-pattern streets, advanced drainage systems, and public buildings. The civilization's economy is based on agriculture, trade, and craft production. The undeciphered script on seals suggests a complex administrative system, though the nature of political organization remains unknown.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The existence and general characteristics of the Indus Valley Civilization are well-established through archaeology. The specific year -2332 is assigned as a representative year within the mature phase.

disconfirming_evidence: The extent of Naram-Sin's Anatolian conquests is debated; some scholars argue that his inscriptions exaggerate the permanence and extent of his control. The Indus Valley Civilization's political organization and the nature of its governance remain speculative due to the undeciphered script.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -2332 is sparse. For Mesopotamia and Egypt, inscriptions and administrative documents provide the primary evidence. For the Indus Valley, the record is archaeological. The record is heavily weighted toward literate civilizations; non-literate societies are underrepresented. Dating precision is limited to ±5–10 years for this period.

graph_edges.note: Anatolian campaigns are part of Naram-Sin's broader expansion
